---
title: "RP 1: Occasions and Drinking Associations"
format: html
---
# RP 1: Occasions and Drinking Associations

Many people associate drinking with "special occasions" or social rituals. The brain starts to pair celebration, relief, or connection with alcohol, which can make events feel risky even when you are committed to sobriety. This is why birthdays, holidays, paydays, and reunions often require extra planning.

Recovery does not mean avoiding all positive experiences. It means creating new rituals so enjoyable occasions remain enjoyable **without** alcohol. Repetition builds new associations over time.

High-risk occasions can also involve group influence effects, where surrounding behavior increases urge intensity. Protective planning works best when you decide boundaries, support contacts, and exit cues before emotional and social pressure rise.

## Occasion Planning Sheet
| Upcoming occasion | Old alcohol pattern | New sober ritual | Backup plan if urge rises |
|---|---|---|---|
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

::: {.callout-note}
### Reflection and action

As you complete this section, link each insight to one specific behavior.

Before your next occasion, decide your arrival time, departure time, and support check-in.

Small repeatable actions are more useful than perfect one-time efforts.
:::
